event: Neolithic communities settle Qatar's coast, leaving Ubaid pottery and evidence of trade links with Mesopotamia.
Coastal populations engage in fishing, pearling, and seasonal habitation along the peninsula.
Bronze Age occupation associated with the Dilmun civilization leaves traces of regional maritime trade.
Rock carvings (petroglyphs) at Al Jassasiya and other sites depict boats, cup-marks, and early maritime culture.
Qatar lies within the trading sphere of the Hellenistic and later Parthian-influenced Gulf, referenced by classical geographers.
The region falls under the influence of the Sasanian Persian Empire, which dominates the Gulf for centuries.
Qatar's inhabitants convert to Islam following the spread of the new faith across eastern Arabia.
Under the Abbasid Caliphate, Qatar prospers as a center for pearl diving, weaving, and Gulf trade.
Murwab and other settlements reflect Islamic-era occupation, though many sites are later abandoned amid regional decline.
Portuguese maritime expansion brings the Gulf coast, including Qatar, into competition among European powers.
The Ottoman Empire asserts nominal authority over eastern Arabia and the Gulf coast.
Bani Khalid tribal confederation from Al-Hasa exerts control over the Qatar peninsula.
Members of the Al Khalifa and Al Jalahima clans migrate to Qatar, settling at Zubarah.
Forces based in Qatar's Zubarah help the Al Khalifa conquer Bahrain, shifting regional power.
Saudi-Wahhabi influence extends into Qatar amid the turbulence of the first Saudi state.
The Al Thani family rises to prominence, establishing the dynasty that still rules Qatar today.
Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani emerges as a leading figure consolidating tribal authority on the peninsula.
Bahraini and Abu Dhabi forces devastate Doha and Al Wakrah, an attack that galvanizes Qatari identity.
Britain brokers a treaty recognizing Mohammed bin Thani, marking Qatar's emergence as a distinct entity.
Qatar comes under Ottoman administration as the Ottomans station a garrison at Doha.
Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani, regarded as Qatar's founder, becomes the de facto ruler.
Qatari forces under Sheikh Jassim defeat an Ottoman expedition at the Battle of Al Wajbah.
The Ottomans relinquish their claims to Qatar following the Anglo-Ottoman Convention.
Qatar becomes a British protectorate as Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim signs a treaty with Britain.
Qatar grants a 75-year oil concession to the Anglo-Persian Oil Company affiliate.
Oil is discovered at the Dukhan field, foreshadowing Qatar's economic transformation.
The collapse of the natural pearl market, undercut by Japanese cultured pearls, devastates Qatar's economy.
Qatar begins its first commercial export of crude oil after a wartime delay.
Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Thani's reign begins a period of rising oil revenues and modernization.
Britain announces its withdrawal from the Gulf, prompting Qatar to consider federation with neighboring states.
Qatar declares full independence on 3 September and joins the United Nations and the Arab League.
Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani seizes power in a bloodless coup, accelerating state development.
Qatar University, the nation's first institution of higher education, is established.
Qatar becomes a founding member of the Gulf Cooperation Council.
Qatari forces participate in the Gulf War coalition that liberates Kuwait, and the Battle of Khafji involves Qatari troops.
Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani deposes his father in a bloodless coup and launches sweeping reforms.
Al Jazeera satellite television network launches from Doha, reshaping Arab media.
Qatar begins large-scale liquefied natural gas exports from the giant North Field, fueling rapid wealth.
Qatar holds its first democratic elections, for a Central Municipal Council, with women voting and standing.
Qatar adopts a new constitution by referendum and hosts US Central Command at Al Udeid Air Base during the Iraq War.
Doha hosts the 15th Asian Games, showcasing Qatar's growing global profile.
Qatar wins the right to host the 2022 FIFA World Cup, the first in the Arab world.
Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani becomes Emir after his father's voluntary abdication.
Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Bahrain, and Egypt impose a diplomatic and economic blockade on Qatar.
Qatar withdraws from OPEC to focus on its dominant position in liquefied natural gas.
The Al-Ula agreement ends the Gulf blockade, and Qatar holds its first legislative Shura Council elections.
Qatar hosts the FIFA World Cup, the first held in the Middle East and the Arab world.
